sections.heading: Understanding Recomposition
sections.content: Recomposition is the process by which Compose re-executes composable functions when their observed state changes. Unlike the View system where you manually update specific views, Compose automatically identifies which composables depend on changed state and re-runs only those functions — leaving unaffected composables untouched. This makes Compose highly efficient, but it also means composable functions can be called many times, in any order, and potentially in parallel. Understanding when and why recomposition happens is essential for building correct and performant Compose UIs.

sections.heading: How Recomposition Works
items: When state observed by a composable changes, Compose schedules that composable for recomposition
Only the composables that read the changed state are recomposed — their siblings and parents are skipped
Recomposition is incremental — Compose compares the new composition with the previous one and applies only the differences
Composables that receive the same inputs as the previous composition may be skipped entirely (smart recomposition)
Recomposition is not guaranteed to happen immediately — Compose batches and schedules recompositions efficiently
Composables can be recomposed in any order — never depend on execution order between sibling composables
Composables can be recomposed in parallel on multiple threads — avoid writing to shared mutable state
Composables can be executed multiple times for a single frame — keep them fast and side-effect free
If a composable's inputs have not changed since the last composition, Compose may skip it — this is called skipping or memoization

rows.description: Any composable that reads a MutableState value recomposes when the value is set to a new value
rows.example: var count by remember { mutableStateOf(0) }; count++ triggers recomposition of composables reading count

rows.description: collectAsState() or collectAsStateWithLifecycle() recomposes the composable when the Flow emits a new value
rows.example: val uiState by viewModel.uiState.collectAsStateWithLifecycle() recomposes when uiState changes

rows.description: If a parent composable recomposes and passes a different value to a child, the child recomposes
rows.example: UserCard(user = user) recomposes UserCard when the parent recomposes with a new user object

rows.description: Adding, removing, or updating items in an observable collection triggers recomposition
derivedStateOf recomposes its readers only when the derived value actually changes, not on every upstream change
rows.example: val isValid by remember { derivedStateOf { email.contains("@") } }

sections.heading: remember and mutableStateOf
sections.content: The two fundamental tools for local state in Compose are remember and mutableStateOf. remember is a composable function that executes its initialization lambda only on the first composition and returns the cached result on all subsequent recompositions. mutableStateOf creates a MutableState holder whose value changes trigger recomposition in any composable that reads it. Together, remember { mutableStateOf(initial) } creates persistent, observable state that lives as long as the composable is in the composition.

sections.heading: remember Patterns and Variants
items: var text by remember { mutableStateOf("") } — local string state, common for TextField value
var isExpanded by remember { mutableStateOf(false) } — local boolean toggle state
val listState = rememberLazyListState() — remembers scroll position of a LazyColumn across recompositions
val coroutineScope = rememberCoroutineScope() — remembers a CoroutineScope bound to the composable's lifecycle
val focusRequester = remember { FocusRequester() } — remembers a non-state object that should not be recreated
remember(key) { } — recalculates the remembered value when key changes: remember(userId) { loadUser(userId) }
remember(key1, key2) { } — recalculates when either key changes
rememberSaveable { mutableStateOf(0) } — like remember but persists across configuration changes via Bundle
rememberSaveable with custom Saver for complex types: rememberSaveable(stateSaver = CustomSaver) { }

rows.rememberSaveable: Yes — saved to Bundle and restored
Yes — if stored value is Bundle-compatible
Bundle-compatible types (String, Int, Boolean, Parcelable) or types with custom Saver

rows.remember: Transient UI state that can reset on rotation: dropdown expanded, animation trigger
rows.rememberSaveable: User input state that should persist on rotation: TextField text, scroll position, selected tab

sections.heading: State Hoisting
sections.content: State hoisting is the Compose pattern of moving state out of a composable and into its caller, making the composable stateless. A stateless composable receives the current state value as a parameter and emits change events via a callback lambda — it owns no state of its own. This pattern makes composables reusable across different contexts, easier to test in isolation, and easier to share state between sibling composables through a common parent. The principle is: state goes down as parameters, events go up as callbacks.

sections.heading: State Hoisting Pattern
items: Stateful version (owns state): @Composable fun Counter() { var count by remember { mutableStateOf(0) }; CounterContent(count, onIncrement = { count++ }) }
Stateless version (hoisted): @Composable fun CounterContent(count: Int, onIncrement: () -> Unit) { Text("$count"); Button(onClick = onIncrement) { Text("+") } }
Hoist state when: two composables need to read the same state
Hoist state when: a parent composable needs to know about a child's state to make decisions
Hoist state when: the composable should be reusable in different contexts with different state sources
The single source of truth principle — each piece of state should have exactly one owner
State flows down (as parameters), events flow up (as lambdas) — unidirectional data flow
In MVVM: hoist state all the way up to the ViewModel; the root screen composable connects ViewModel state to stateless child composables

rows.stateful: Owns and manages its own state with remember
rows.stateless: Receives state as parameters; owns no state

rows.stateful: Lower — state logic is coupled to the composable
rows.stateless: Higher — can be driven by any state source

rows.stateful: Harder — must trigger state changes to test different appearances
rows.stateless: Easy — pass different parameter values to test all states
Can preview any state by passing different parameters

rows.stateful: Top-level screen composables that own state; entry points that connect to ViewModel
rows.stateless: Reusable UI components: buttons, cards, list items, inputs

rows.stateful: HomeScreen() — collects ViewModel state and passes to child composables
rows.stateless: UserCard(user: User, onFavorite: (User) -> Unit) — pure UI, no state

sections.heading: Side Effects in Compose
sections.content: A side effect is any operation that affects state outside the composable's own scope — launching a coroutine, subscribing to a flow, showing a snackbar, logging analytics, or updating external state. Because composable functions can be re-executed at any time, performing side effects directly in the composable body can cause them to run multiple times unexpectedly. Compose provides a family of effect handlers — LaunchedEffect, SideEffect, DisposableEffect, produceState, and derivedStateOf — that run side effects at the correct point in the composition lifecycle.

rows.when_runs: Runs when the composable enters composition and re-runs when key changes; cancelled when composable leaves
rows.use_for: Launching coroutines from a composable: showing a snackbar, navigating, loading data on first render
Pushing Compose state into non-Compose code that doesn't support State: analytics, logging, updating external objects

rows.when_runs: Runs when composable enters composition; onDispose block runs when composable leaves or key changes
rows.use_for: Registering and unregistering listeners, callbacks, or resources that need explicit cleanup

rows.when_runs: Returns a CoroutineScope tied to the composable's lifetime — call from event handlers
rows.use_for: Launching coroutines in response to user events (button clicks) where LaunchedEffect is not appropriate
rows.example: val scope = rememberCoroutineScope(); Button(onClick = { scope.launch { scrollState.animateScrollTo(0) } })

rows.when_runs: Runs a coroutine in composition; produces State<T> values from async sources
rows.use_for: Converting non-Compose async sources (callbacks, Flows) into Compose State
rows.example: val image by produceState<Bitmap?>(null) { value = loadImage(url) }

rows.when_runs: Recalculates only when the state values it reads change — not on every recomposition
rows.use_for: Expensive calculations derived from state that should not recompute on every recomposition
rows.example: val showButton by remember { derivedStateOf { listState.firstVisibleItemIndex > 0 } }

sections.heading: LaunchedEffect — Key Patterns
items: LaunchedEffect(Unit) — runs once when the composable enters composition; Unit never changes so it never re-runs
LaunchedEffect(userId) — re-runs whenever userId changes; previous coroutine is cancelled first
LaunchedEffect(true) — equivalent to LaunchedEffect(Unit) — runs once
LaunchedEffect(viewModel) — re-runs if the ViewModel instance changes (rarely needed)
Collect a one-time event channel: LaunchedEffect(Unit) { viewModel.events.collect { event -> handleEvent(event) } }
Show a snackbar: LaunchedEffect(snackbarMessage) { if (snackbarMessage != null) snackbarHostState.showSnackbar(snackbarMessage) }
Load data on entry: LaunchedEffect(Unit) { viewModel.loadData() }
LaunchedEffect is cancelled and restarted when its key changes — use this for dependent data loading

rows.derived_state_of: Computes a value derived from other state; only recomposes readers when derived value changes
rows.remember_key: Recomputes the remembered value when the key itself changes

rows.derived_state_of: When any state it reads changes — but only notifies readers if the result is different
rows.remember_key: When the key parameter changes value

rows.derived_state_of: Reducing recompositions from frequently changing state: scroll position, filter results
rows.remember_key: Recalculating an object when an ID or input changes: remember(userId) { UserPresenter(userId) }

rows.derived_state_of: val showFab by remember { derivedStateOf { listState.firstVisibleItemIndex > 0 } }

sections.heading: Connecting ViewModel State to Compose
items: Expose state from ViewModel as StateFlow<UiState>: val uiState: StateFlow<UiState> = ... .stateIn(viewModelScope, WhileSubscribed(5000), UiState.Loading)
Collect in composable: val uiState by viewModel.uiState.collectAsStateWithLifecycle()
collectAsStateWithLifecycle() is preferred over collectAsState() — it stops collecting when the composable is not active (lifecycle-aware)
Add dependency: implementation("androidx.lifecycle:lifecycle-runtime-compose:2.x.x")
For one-time events (navigation, snackbar): use a Channel<Event> in ViewModel and collect with LaunchedEffect
Pass the ViewModel to the top-level screen composable only; pass individual state and callbacks to child composables
Never pass the ViewModel itself deep into the composable tree — hoist state and pass lambdas instead
Use hiltViewModel() to obtain a Hilt-injected ViewModel inside a composable: val viewModel: MyViewModel = hiltViewModel()

rows.description: sealed class UiState { object Loading : UiState(); data class Success(val data: List<User>) : UiState(); data class Error(val message: String) : UiState() }
val uiState: StateFlow<UiState> = repo.getUsers().map { UiState.Success(it) }.catch { emit(UiState.Error(it.message ?: "")) }.stateIn(viewModelScope, WhileSubscribed(5000), UiState.Loading)
when (uiState) { is UiState.Loading -> CircularProgressIndicator(); is UiState.Success -> UserList(uiState.data); is UiState.Error -> ErrorMessage(uiState.message) }

sections.heading: Performance — Avoiding Unnecessary Recompositions
items: Move state reads as low as possible in the composable tree — only the composables that actually use the state will recompose
Use derivedStateOf for values derived from high-frequency state like scroll position
Avoid creating new lambda instances in the composable body — use remember { } or define them outside
Use stable data classes as composable parameters — Compose can skip recomposition if all parameters are equal
Annotate data classes with @Stable or @Immutable to help Compose's compiler skip recomposition
Avoid passing unstable types (List, Map) directly as parameters — wrap in a @Stable class or use ImmutableList
Use the Compose compiler metrics to identify composables that are not skippable
key() composable — provides a unique key for items in a list to help Compose correctly track additions, removals, and moves

sections.heading: Common State and Recomposition Mistakes to Avoid

rows.mistake: Reading state outside a composable (in a non-composable function)
rows.fix: State can only be read inside a composable function — pass state values as parameters to non-composable functions

rows.mistake: Writing to state directly in the composable body during composition
rows.fix: Never write to state during composition — update state only in response to events or in LaunchedEffect

rows.mistake: Using remember without mutableStateOf for values that should trigger recomposition
rows.fix: remember alone does not trigger recomposition — use remember { mutableStateOf(value) } for reactive state

rows.mistake: Using mutableStateOf without remember — state resets on every recomposition
rows.fix: Always pair mutableStateOf with remember: var x by remember { mutableStateOf(0) }

rows.mistake: Passing a List directly as a composable parameter
rows.fix: List is unstable — Compose always recomposes; use ImmutableList from kotlinx.collections.immutable or wrap in @Stable class

rows.mistake: Collecting StateFlow with collectAsState() instead of collectAsStateWithLifecycle()
rows.fix: collectAsStateWithLifecycle() is lifecycle-aware and stops collection when the app is backgrounded — saves battery and resources

rows.mistake: Launching coroutines directly in the composable body
rows.fix: Use LaunchedEffect for coroutines that run on composition; use rememberCoroutineScope().launch for event-triggered coroutines

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: State and recomposition are the beating heart of Jetpack Compose. Recomposition automatically updates the UI whenever observed state changes, executing only the affected composables for efficiency. Use remember { mutableStateOf() } for local transient state, rememberSaveable for state that should survive rotation, and state hoisting to make composables reusable and testable. Side effects must be managed through the appropriate effect handler — LaunchedEffect for coroutines, DisposableEffect for resource cleanup, and derivedStateOf for reducing unnecessary recompositions. Connect ViewModel state with collectAsStateWithLifecycle() and pass individual state values and event callbacks down the composable tree rather than the ViewModel itself.
